Curriculum → Data structures and algorithms interview prep → DSA foundations and warm-ups → Trees, heaps, and intro dynamic programming

Lesson 3 of 3

Trees, heaps, and intro dynamic programming

Rehearse recursive traversal, state propagation, priority-based selection, and small DP recurrences.

30-45 min DSA flow DSA foundations and warm-ups 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Once an interviewer moves beyond arrays, they are often testing whether you can preserve state across recursion and build a concise recurrence without overcomplicating the code.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.2. Practice set Climbing Stairs · Easy · Dynamic Programming3. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Once an interviewer moves beyond arrays, they are often testing whether you can preserve state across recursion and build a concise recurrence without overcomplicating the code. 2. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.3. Practice set Climbing Stairs · Easy · Dynamic Programming4. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Once an interviewer moves beyond arrays, they are often testing whether you can preserve state across recursion and build a concise recurrence without overcomplicating the code.

What to say first

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
2

Study section

Pattern lens

Use these prompts to practice DFS versus BFS selection, heap usage for top-k style problems, and the moment a recursive relation becomes a DP table or memoized search.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the core data structure choice before you code.
  • State the target time and space complexity up front.
  • Use one invariant or pointer relationship to guide the implementation.
3

Study section

Practice set

Use this cluster to rehearse the same solving pattern across multiple prompts. Start with Climbing Stairs, Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock, Maximum Subarray and keep your explanation focused on why the pattern applies.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Climbing Stairs · Easy · Dynamic Programming
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock · Easy · Dynamic Programming
  • Maximum Subarray · Easy · Dynamic Programming
  • House Robber · Easy · Dynamic Programming
  • Jump Game · Medium · Dynamic Programming
  • Unique Paths · Medium · Dynamic Programming
4

Study section

Execution focus

State what each recursive call returns, what global or accumulated state exists, and how you avoid recomputing overlapping subproblems.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
  • Call out the edge case that is most likely to break the first draft.
  • Finish by restating the complexity and one possible follow-up optimization.

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
  • Test at least one edge case and one representative happy path aloud.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Jumping into code without naming the dominant pattern or invariant.
  • Optimizing too early instead of getting the first correct version working.
  • Ignoring duplicates, empty inputs, or off-by-one boundaries until the end.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What information should a tree DFS return to its caller versus keep globally?
  • When is a heap cleaner than sorting the whole input?
  • How do you know a recursive solution should become memoized dynamic programming?

Coding practice

DSA interview workspace

This lab only surfaces runnable DSA prompts from the lesson set and keeps the editor in a single-file interview format.

WASM runtime idle 6 runnable questions

Problem statement

Climbing Stairs

Easy Dynamic Programming

You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top.

Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?

 

Example 1:

Input: n = 2
Output: 2
Explanation: There are two ways to climb to the top.
1. 1 step + 1 step
2. 2 steps

Example 2:

Input: n = 3
Output: 3
Explanation: There are three ways to climb to the top.
1. 1 step + 1 step + 1 step
2. 1 step + 2 steps
3. 2 steps + 1 step

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= n <= 45

Hints

  • To reach nth step, what could have been your previous steps? (Think about the step sizes)

Runtime model

Runs in-browser with the bundled Pyodide WebAssembly runtime.

Ln 1, Col 1 3 lines 8 words 0.1 KB python

Test case

Run result

Run the selected test case to compare your output against the expected result.
